items.title: IRS settlement preventing Trump-related audits, controversial fund tough to challenge in court, experts say
items.summary_auto: Opponents of President Donald Trump's sweeping legal settlement with the U.S. Internal Revenue Service will face high hurdles in challenging its $1.776-billion US fund for victims of alleged political "weaponization" and its provision barring audits of his taxes, according to legal experts.

items.title: Queen Elizabeth pressed for Andrew to be U.K. trade envoy, documents reveal
items.summary_auto: Documents show Queen Elizabeth was "very keen" that Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, the former prince, be given the job of U.K. trade envoy, as the U.K. government on Thursday released the confidential papers related to his appointment in 2001, which later proved controversial due to his links to American sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

items.title: Israel deports all Gaza flotilla activists after outcry over their detention
items.summary_auto: Gaza activists who were detained by Israel, and later pinned to the ground to the taunts of the country's far-right police minister, have been released from prison and sent home.

items.title: N.B. woman gives birth on the side of the highway due to hospital service disruption
items.summary_auto: A couple is calling for change after they were diverted from their local hospital in Waterville to Fredericton, and their child was born on the side of the Trans-Canada Highway.

items.title: My phone was one of thousands snatched in London this year. Like many others, it was trafficked to China
items.summary_auto: London's Metropolitan Police are trying to crack down on sophisticated criminal networks that are snatching phones off the streets of London and exporting them in bulk to China.

items.title: What does Colbert's exit mean for the future of late night?
items.summary_auto: After 11 seasons, the curtains are closing on The Late Show with Stephen Colbert. Industry watchers say while Colbert's voice will be missed, the late-night format needs to change to survive in the streaming era — a transformation that may already be underway.

items.title: Incarceration rates for women have doubled in the last 2 decades. Why is that?
items.summary_auto: An Edmonton prison for men is being turned into a women's institution, as the number of incarcerated women in Canada has risen and there's an expected shortfall of more than 300 beds over the next five years.

items.title: What tech CEOs want from the new federal AI strategy
items.summary_auto: As the federal government finishes its delayed national AI strategy, some technology leaders in Canada are hopeful the new policy will resemble an industrial blueprint that illustrates Ottawa is serious about rolling up its sleeves to help the sector build in expedient fashion.

items.title: Trump got revenge on his Republican critics. It could come back to bite him
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump is celebrating back-to-back victories in his push to oust his critics from the Republican Party. But there are clear signs that his revenge campaign could backfire.

items.title: Cuba braces for possible U.S. military action as uncertainty follows Castro indictment
items.summary_auto: Former Cuban president Raúl Castro will be "keeping his head down," experts say, as questions swirl over whether the U.S. plans to take him by force and bring him to American soil to face charges over an incident from three decades ago. Federal prosecutors announced criminal charges against Castro Wednesday in the 1996 downing of two small planes flown by Miami-based exile group Brothers to the Rescue.

items.title: Dr. Strangelove diplomacy: How the Pentagon’s symbolic defence board freeze with Canada could backfire
items.summary_auto: Washington’s decision to suspend a symbolic Cold War-era defence body may have been meant to pressure Canada on military policy. Instead, experts say it risks reinforcing Canadians' distrust of the United States, complicates the politics of NORAD and missile defence, and makes future purchases of American military equipment even harder for Ottawa to sell domestically.

items.title: Northern Ontario health authority's ex-CEO, former chief of staff raise concerns after baby's death
items.summary_auto: A former CEO for the Weeneebayko Area Health Authority says the organization had achieved accreditation before he left in 2010 and he's disappointed it's no longer in place, especially after hearing of the death of an infant at the Fort Albany Hospital. "It really hits home when it’s a young boy that’s not even a year old that passes,” Pat Chilton told CBC News.

items.title: U.S.-bound flight from Paris diverts to Montreal over Ebola restrictions
items.summary_auto: An Air France flight from Paris to Detroit was diverted to Montreal Wednesday night after United States authorities barred it from entering the U.S., citing new restrictions for people who who have recently travelled to three East Africa countries, where a deadly Ebola outbreak has taken hold.

items.title: #TheMoment a Sask. couple saved their neighbours from a house fire
items.summary_auto: Samantha Tegart tells The National about the moment she and her boyfriend Greg Stewart saved eight people and a dog from a burning house across the street in Radville, Sask.

items.title: Montreal Victoire win Walter Cup, beating Ottawa 4-0 to become 1st Canadian PWHL champion
items.summary_auto: Abby Roque scored twice to lead the Montreal Victoire to the team's first Walter Cup trophy Wednesday night with a 4-0 victory over the Ottawa Charge.

items.title: Canada’s questions for the Artemis II crew
items.summary_auto: The Artemis II crew (Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, Jeremy Hansen) meets up with CBC News chief correspondent Adrienne Arsenault in Montreal to answer questions from Canadians about their historic million-kilometre mission around the moon.

items.title: Grizzly feeding videos and black bear attack prompt bear safety reminders in B.C.
items.summary_auto: B.C. 's Conservation Officer Service is reminding people to avoid attracting bears into human habitat or luring them with food, after a pair of incidents in the province this week.

items.title: Bear killed in Mission following attack; Grizzlies allegedly being fed near Smithers
items.summary_auto: B.C. 's Conservation Officer Service is reminding people to avoid attracting bears into human habitat or luring them with food, after a pair of incidents in the province this week.

items.title: Did the Artemis II crew get on each other's nerves?
items.summary_auto: During their visit to Canada, CBC News chief correspondent Adrienne Arsenault asked the Artemis II crew about how they managed friction points working together in a challenging environment.

items.title: Just for Laughs founder Gilbert Rozon to pay $930K to 9 women after sexual assault ruling
items.summary_auto: Gilbert Rozon has agreed to pay $930,000 to nine women who accused him of sexual misconduct and has waived his right to appeal the Quebec court ruling that found he sexually assaulted eight of them.

items.title: Gunshots fired at Cricket Canada president's house in Surrey, B.C.
items.summary_auto: The home of the newly elected president of Cricket Canada in Surrey, B.C., was the target of a shooting early Wednesday morning, the fifth estate has learned.

items.title: B.C. man trapped upside-down in truck for 2 days rescued and hospitalized
items.summary_auto: The man was driving on a remote forest service road Sunday when the vehicle rolled down a steep embankment. He remained trapped inside, unable to access food or water, until he was reported missing by family. He was rescued two days later and was taken to hospital in serious condition.

items.title: Elon Musk's SpaceX plans IPO with shares to be sold as soon as June
items.summary_auto: SpaceX took the wraps off its IPO filing on Wednesday, opening the books of the company that has already revolutionized rocket technology, with even larger ambitions to colonize Mars and build AI data centres in space.

items.title: As rare Ebola virus spreads in Congo, residents say 'nobody knows whether they are safe'
items.summary_auto: As health officials scramble to respond to a virus that has spread undetected for weeks, or possibly months, people in Congo are gripped with fear.

items.title: CFIA suspends licences of Quebec frozen dessert maker
items.summary_auto: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) suspended two Safe Food for Canadians (SFC) licences held by Abe's Frozen Desserts Inc. on May 8, according to a government news release.

items.title: Ontario resident being tested for Ebola after travel to East Africa, says Health Ministry
items.summary_auto: An Ontario resident who recently travelled to East Africa is being tested for Ebola, according to the province's Health Ministry.

items.title: Trump's new $1.776B 'Anti-Weaponization Fund' is a wild ride | About That
items.summary_auto: The Trump administration set up a roughly $1.8 billion US fund to support Americans facing domestic political persecution — but there are questions about accountability and who may benefit most from the cash. Andrew Chang explains how the 'anti-weaponization' fund originates with U.S. President Donald Trump's own lawsuit, and the concerns about a conflict of interest. (Photo credits: The Canadian Press, Reuters, Adobe Stock and Getty Images)

items.title: Federal officials on the defensive as momentum grows against lawful access bill
items.summary_auto: Canada's spy agency says its ability to keep pace with threats and contribute to intelligence alliances will be at risk if the government's latest attempt to pass a lawful access bill fails — a warning that comes as momentum against the Liberals' Bill C-22 grows.

items.title: Banff grizzly cub struck by train in 'heartbreaking' loss for sensitive species
items.summary_auto: A grizzly bear cub has been struck and killed by a train in Banff National Park, where Parks Canada has recorded 19 grizzly deaths on tracks since 2005.

items.title: Ukraine beefs up northern defences, points to risks of new Russian offensive north of Kyiv
items.summary_auto: Ukraine will send reinforcements to its northern regions and step up diplomatic pressure on Belarus to counter what Kyiv believes are Russian plans to launch a new offensive north of ‌the capital, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said.

items.title: 5 things to know as Habs, Hurricanes face off in Eastern Conference final
items.summary_auto: The Montreal Canadiens and Carolina Hurricanes will face off in the third round of the Stanley Cup playoffs after taking completely different routes to get there.

items.title: Alberta legislative committee on pro-Canada petition adjourns after prematurely sent news release
items.summary_auto: A legislative committee deciding what to do about a pro-Canada petition descended into procedural chaos Wednesday after the UCP caucus sent out a news release that presumed the outcome of a vote that hadn’t yet happened.

items.title: Amelia Earhart is back in Harbour Grace, N.L., as stolen statue returns home
items.summary_auto: Ninety-four years after Ameila Earhart left Harbour Grace to embark on her historic solo flight across the Atlantic — and about a year after a statue honouring her was stolen — her bronze feet are now back on the ground in the community.

items.title: 2 Alberta cabinet ministers stepping down
items.summary_auto: Alberta Finance Minister Nate Horner and Minister of Hospital and Surgical Health Services Matt Jones have both announced they will not seek re-election in advance of a cabinet shuffle Thursday.

items.title: Canadian mining company Sherritt signs preliminary deal to sell majority stake to Gillon Capital
items.summary_auto: Sherritt suspended direct participation in its joint venture in Cuba earlier this month amid ramped up pressure by the U.S. on the island nation.
